content.problemStatement:
The airport in Chefland is a busy place! Today, we'll take a look at its security queues.

There are NNN security queues, numbered 111 to NNN. Initially, there are AiA_iAi​ people standing in the iii-th queue.

You are the last person in the the first queue, i.e. the A1A_1A1​-th person in queue 111.

Every second, the following will happen:


First, you will either stay in the same queue, or move to an adjacent queue.

That is, if you are in queue iii at the start of the second, you can either stay in queue iii, or move to one of queue i−1i-1i−1 or queue i+1i+1i+1 (only if the corresponding queue exists, i.e. you cannot move to queue i+1i+1i+1 when i=Ni = Ni=N or move to queue i−1i-1i−1 when i=1i = 1i=1).

If you move to a new queue, you will be the last person in it.
Then, the first person in every queue will complete their security check and leave.
Naturally, you'd like to finish your security check as soon as possible.

What's the minimum number of seconds you'll need if you act optimally?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of queues.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ – the number of people in each queue initially.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum number of seconds you'll need to complete your security check.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal sequence of moves is as follows:

Second 111: Move to the second queue. The counts of people are now [4,3,1,3][4, 3, 1, 3][4,3,1,3].
The first person in each queue leaves, so the counts are [3,2,0,2][3, 2, 0, 2][3,2,0,2].
Second 222: Move to the third queue. The counts of people are now [3,1,1,2][3, 1, 1, 2][3,1,1,2].
The first person in each queue leaves, so the counts are [2,0,0,1][2, 0, 0, 1][2,0,0,1].
We were the first person in the third queue, meaning we've completed our security check and are done.

So, we were able to leave in two seconds.
Test case 222: The queue counts are [4,5][4, 5][4,5], with us being in the first queue.
The optimal path is to just wait in the first queue without moving.
Since one person leaves the queue every second, we'll leave by the 444-th second.
Test case 333: One optimal solution is as follows:

Wait in queue 111 for 333 seconds.
In the fourth second, move to queue 222.
We'll be the first person in queue 222 now and so leave (the three people initially in it would have left after the first three seconds).
